volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 3.3.747 - Section 4.2.866)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 3.3.747
sections.original: Nam hercle omnia istaec veniunt in mentem mihi .
sections.translation: All these very same things suggest themselves to my mind .

sections.label: Section 3.3.748
sections.original: vide si hóc utibile magis atque in rem deputas ,
sections.translation: Consider if you think this more feasible and more to the purpose : go to the young man himself , and tell him how the matter really stands .

sections.label: Section 3.3.750
sections.original: sed ut égo nunc adulescenti thensaurum indicem
sections.translation: Should I now discover the treasure to a young man , ill-regulated , and brimful of passion and of wantonness ? No , faith , most assuredly , by no means . For I know , beyond a doubt , that he would devour even all that spot where it is buried . I fear to dig for it , lest he should hear the noise ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.755
sections.original: neu rem ipsam indaget , dotem dare si dixerim .
sections.translation: lest , too , he might trace out the matter itself , if I should say I will give her a portion .

sections.label: Section 3.3.756
sections.original: Quo pacto ergo igitur clam dos depromi potest ?
sections.translation: By what method , then , can the portion be secretly taken out ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.757
sections.original: Dum occasio ei rei reperiatur , interim
sections.translation: Until an opportunity can be found for that business , I would , in the meanwhile , ask for a loan of the money from some friend or other .

sections.label: Section 3.3.759
sections.original: Potin est ab amico alicunde exorari ?
sections.translation: Can it be obtained from some friend or other ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.759b
sections.original: Potest .
sections.translation: It can .

sections.label: Section 3.3.760
sections.original: Gerrae . ne tu illud verbum actutum inveneris :
sections.translation: Nonsense ; you ’ ll certainly meet with this answer at once : O , upon my faith , I really have not anything that I can lend you .

sections.label: Section 3.3.762
sections.original: Malim hercle ut verum dicas , quam ut des mutuom .
sections.translation: Troth , I would rather they would tell me the truth than lend me the money with a bad grace .

sections.label: Section 3.3.763
sections.original: Sed vide consilium , si placet .
sections.translation: But consider this plan , if it pleases you .

sections.label: Section 3.3.763b
sections.original: Quid consilist ?
sections.translation: What is the plan ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.764
sections.original: Scitum , ut ego opinor , consilium inveni .
sections.translation: I have found out a clever plan , as I think .

sections.label: Section 3.3.764b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.765
sections.original: Homo cónducatur aliquis iam , quantum potest ,
sections.translation: Let some person , now , be hired ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.766b
sections.original: Quid is scit facere postea ?
sections.translation: What is he to understand that he must do after that ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.768
sections.original: ignota facie , quae hic non visitata sit ;
sections.translation: of an appearance as much unknown as possible , such as has not been often seen .

sections.label: Section 3.3.769
sections.original: mendacilocum aliquem
sections.translation: It is necessary for him to be some lying ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.769b
sections.original: Quid is scit facere postea ?
sections.translation: And what then , after that ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.770
sections.original: falsidicum , confidentem .
sections.translation: deceiving , impudent fellow — a lounger from the Forum .

sections.label: Section 3.3.767
sections.original: Is homo exornetur graphice in peregrinum modum ;
sections.translation: Let this person be dressed up to the life after a foreign fashion , just as though he were a foreigner .

sections.label: Section 3.3.771
sections.original: quasi ad adulescentem a patre ex Seleucia
sections.translation: Let him come to the young man as though from Seleucia , from his father ; let him pronounce his salutation to him in the words of his father , say that he is prospering in business , and is alive and well , and that he will be shortly coming back again . Let him bring two letters ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.775
sections.original: duas , éas nos consignemus , quasi sint a patre :
sections.translation: let us seal these , as though they are from his father . Let him give the one to him , and let him say that he wishes to give the other to yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.3.777b
sections.original: Perge porro dicere .
sections.translation: Go on , and tell me still further .

sections.label: Section 3.3.778
sections.original: Seque aurum ferre virgini dotem a patre
sections.translation: Let him say that he is bringing some gold as a marriage-portion from her father for the girl , and that his father has requested him to deliver it to you .

sections.label: Section 3.3.780
sections.original: tenes iam ?
sections.translation: Do you understand me now ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.780b
sections.original: Propemodo , atque ausculto perlubens .
sections.translation: Pretty nearly ; and I listen with great satisfaction .

sections.label: Section 3.3.781
sections.original: Tum tú igitur demum adulescenti aurum dabis ,
sections.translation: Then , in consequence , you will finally give the gold to the young man when the girl shall be given in marriage .

sections.label: Section 3.3.783
sections.original: Scite hercle sane .
sections.translation: Troth , ’ tis very cleverly contrived .

sections.label: Section 3.3.783b
sections.original: Hoc , ubi thensaurum effoderis ,
sections.translation: By this means , when you have dug up the treasure , you will have removed all cause for suspicion from the young man .

sections.label: Section 3.3.785
sections.original: censebit aurum esse a patre allatum tibi ,
sections.translation: He will think that the gold has been brought to you from his father ; whereas , you will be taking it from the treasure .

sections.label: Section 3.3.786b
sections.original: Satis scite et probe ;
sections.translation: Very cleverly and fairly contrived ; although I am ashamed , at this time of life , for me to be playing a double part . But when he shall bring the letters sealed , don ’ t you suppose that the young man will then

sections.label: Section 3.3.790
sections.original: paterni signum novisse ?
sections.translation: recollect the impression of his father ’ s signet [*] ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.790b
sections.original: Etiam tu taces ?
sections.translation: Will you be silent now ? Reasons innumerable may be found for that circumstance . That which he used to have he has lost , and he has since had another new one made . Then , if he should bring them not sealed at all , this might be said ,— that they had been unsealed for him by the custom-house officers [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.795
sections.original: inspectasque esse . in huius modi negotio
sections.translation: and had been examined . On matters of this kind , however , ’ tis mere idleness to spend the day in talk ; although a long discussion might be spun out . Go now , at once , privately to the treasure ; send to a distance the men-servants and the maids ; and — do you hear ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.799b
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: What is it ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.800
sections.original: Vxorem quoque eampse hanc rem uti celes face ,
sections.translation: Take care that you conceal this matter from that same wife of yours as well ; for , i ’ faith , there is never any subject which they can be silent upon . Why are you standing now ? Why don ’ t you take yourself off hence , and bestir yourself ? Open the treasure , take thence as much gold as is requisite for this purpose ; at once close it up again , but secretly ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.805
sections.original: sicut praecepi ; cunctos exturba aedibus .
sections.translation: as I have enjoined you ; turn all out of the house .

sections.label: Section 3.3.806
sections.original: Ita faciam .
sections.translation: I will do so .

sections.label: Section 3.3.806b
sections.original: At énim nimis lóngo sermone utimur ,
sections.translation: But , really , we are continuing too long a discourse ; we are wasting the day , whereas there is need now of all expedition . There is nothing for you to fear about the seal ; trust me for that . This is a clever excuse to give , as I mentioned ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.810
sections.original: apud pórtitores esse inspectas . denique
sections.translation: that they have been looked at by the officers . In fine , don ’ t you see the time of day ? What do you think of him being of such a nature and disposition ? He is drunk already ; anything you like may be proved for him . Besides , what is the greatest point of all , this person will say that he brings , and not that he applies for , money .

sections.label: Section 3.3.814b
sections.original: Iam sat est .
sections.translation: Now , that ’ s enough .

sections.label: Section 3.3.815
sections.original: Ego sycophantam iam conduco de foro
sections.translation: I am now going to hire a sharper [*] from the Forum , and then I will seal the two letters ; and I ’ ll send him thither ( pointing to the house of CHARMIDES ) , well tutored in his part , to this young man .

sections.label: Section 3.3.818b
sections.original: Eo ego igitur intro ad officium meum .
sections.translation: I am going in-doors then to my duty in consequence . Do you see about this matter .

sections.label: Section 3.3.819b
sections.original: Actum reddam nugacissume .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll take care it ’ s done in the very cleverest style .

sections.label: Section 4.1.820
sections.original: Salsípotenti et mulsípotenti Iovis frátri et Nerei Néptuno
sections.translation: To Neptune , potent o ’ er the deep and most powerful , the brother of aethereal Jove , joyously and sincerely do I proffer praise , and return my grateful thanks ; to the salt waves , too , with whom lay supreme power over myself ,— one , too , that existed over my property and my life , — inasmuch as from their realms they have returned me safe and sound even to my own native city . And , Neptune , before the other Deities , do I both give and return to you extreme thanks .

sections.label: Section 4.1.825
sections.original: nam te ómnes saevomqué severumque atque ávidis moribus cómmemorant ,
sections.translation: For all people talk of you as being cruel and severe , of voracious habits , filthy , unsightly , unendurable , and outrageous ; on the other hand , I have experienced your kindly aid . For , in good sooth , I have found you mild and merciful upon the deep , even to that degree that I wished . This commendation , too , I had already heard with these ears before of you among men ,— that you were accustomed to spare the poor , and to depress and overawe the rich .

sections.label: Section 4.1.830
sections.original: abi , laúdo , scis ordíne , ut aequomst , tractáre homines ; hoc dís dignumst .
sections.translation: Adieu ! I commend you ; you know how to treat men properly , according as is just . This is worthy of the Gods ; they should ever prove benignant to the needy ; to men of high station , quite otherwise . Trusty have you proved , though they are in the habit of saying that you cannot be trusted . For , without you , it would have happened , I am very sure , that on the deep your attendants would have shockingly torn in pieces and rent asunder wretched me , and , together with me , my property as well , in every direction throughout the azure surface of ocean .

sections.label: Section 4.1.835
sections.original: ita iám quasi canes , haud sécus , circum stabánt navem turbinés venti ,
sections.translation: But just now , like raging dogs , and no otherwise , did the winds in hurricane beset the ship ; storms and waves , and raging squalls were about to roar , to break the mast , to bear down the yards , to split the sails ; had not your favouring kindness been nigh at hand . Have done with me , if you please ; henceforth have I now determined to give myself up to ease ; enough have I got . With what pains have I struggled , while I was acquiring riches for my son .

sections.label: Section 4.1.840
sections.original: sed quís hic est , qui in plateam íngreditur
sections.translation: But who is this [*] that is coming up the street with his new-fangled garb and appearance ? I ’ faith , though I wish to be at home , I ’ ll wait awhile ; at the same time , I will give my attention to see what business this fellow is about .

sections.label: Section 4.2.843
sections.original: Huíc ego die nomén Trinummo fáciam : nam ego operám meam
sections.translation: To this day I give the name of The Festival of the Three Pieces ( Trinummus ); for , on this day , have I let out my services in a cheating scheme for three pieces of money .

sections.label: Section 4.2.845
sections.original: advenio ex Seleucia , Macedonia , Asia atque Arabia ,
sections.translation: I am just arrived from Seleucia , Macedonia , Asia , and Arabia ,— places which I never visited either with my eye or with my foot . See now , what business poverty brings upon the man that is wretchedly destitute ; inasmuch as I am now obliged , for the sake of three pieces of money , to say that I received these letters from a certain person , about whom I don ’ t know ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.850
sections.original: neque novi , neque natus necne is fuerit , id solide scio .
sections.translation: nor have I ever known , who the man is , nor do I know this for certain , whether he was ever born or not .

sections.label: Section 4.2.851
sections.original: Pol hic quidem fungino generest : capite se totum tegit .
sections.translation: Faith , this fellow ’ s surely of the mushroom genus ; he covers himself entirely with his top . [*] The countenance of the fellow appears to be Illyrian ; he comes , too , in that garb .

sections.label: Section 4.2.853
sections.original: Ille qui mé conduxit , ubi conduxit , abduxit domum ,
sections.translation: He who hired me , when he had hired me , took me to his house ; he told me what he wanted to be done ; he taught and showed me beforehand

sections.label: Section 4.2.855
sections.original: quo modo quicque agerem ; nunc adeo si quid ego addidero amplius ,
sections.translation: how I was to do everything . If , then , I should add anything more , my employer will on that account the better forward his plan through me . As he dressed me out , so am I now equipped ; his money did that . He himself borrowed my costume , at his own risk , from the theatrical wardrobe [*] ; if I shall be able , now , to impose on this man through my garb ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.860
sections.original: dabo operam , ut me esse ipsum plane sycophantam sentiat .
sections.translation: I will give him occasion clearly to find that I am a very trickster .

sections.label: Section 4.2.861
sections.original: Quam magis spécto , minus placet mi haec hominis facies . mira sunt ,
sections.translation: The more I look at him , the less does the appearance of the fellow please me . ’ Tis a wonder if that fellow there is not either a night-robber [*] or a cutpurse . He is viewing the locality ; he is looking around him and surveying the houses ; troth , I think he is reconnoitring the spot for him to come and rob bye and bye .

sections.label: Section 4.2.865
sections.original: magis lubidost opservare quid agat : ei rei operam dabo .
sections.translation: I have a still greater desire to watch what he is about : I ’ ll give attention to this matter .

sections.label: Section 4.2.866
sections.original: Has regiones demonstravit mi ille conductor meus ;
sections.translation: This employer of mine pointed out these localities to me ; at this house are my devices to be put in practice . I ’ ll knock at the door .
